title: Dynamical susceptibility and quantum Fisher information in the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model with Hatsugai-Kohmoto interactions
abstract: We investigate the dynamical spin and charge susceptibilities and the associated quantum Fisher information in a class of interacting lattice models, with a primary focus on the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model in the presence of Hatsugai-Kohmoto interactions. To provide a rigorous analytical benchmark, we contrast the response properties of the SSH-HK system with those of the single-band Hubbard and SSH-Hubbard models, treated within the random-phase approximation. While standard Hubbard-type interactions typically suppress excitation strength, we demonstrate that the SSH-HK model displays qualitatively distinct physical behavior arising from the interplay between SSH dimerization and the momentum-diagonal nature of the HK interaction. Leveraging the exact solvability of the HK term, we derive closed-form expressions for the dynamical susceptibility, revealing unique filling-controlled characteristics such as a finite response at zero wave vector and a pronounced restructuring of spectral weight across integer and fractional filling sectors. We show that the quantum Fisher information, defined as the frequency integral of the imaginary part of the susceptibility, serves as an efficient probe of these filling sectors, exhibiting distinct piecewise behavior that distinguishes integer from fractional fillings. Notably, our results indicate that the quantum Fisher information remains insensitive to topological transitions within uniform-density regimes, highlighting the limitations of standard dynamical response functions in characterizing band topology. These findings establish the SSH-HK model as a powerful analytical platform for exploring the competition between topology and strong correlations, demonstrating how dynamical susceptibilities and the quantum Fisher information provide complementary, experimentally accessible probes of many-body physics.
summary: This paper explores the dynamical properties of the SSH model when augmented with long-range Hatsugai-Kohmoto interactions. It demonstrates that while the Quantum Fisher Information can effectively distinguish between different particle filling sectors, it fails to detect topological phase transitions in uniform-density regimes. The work highlights how the interplay between dimerization and specific interaction types can create unique spectral features not found in standard Hubbard models.
main_problem: The study investigates the dynamical response properties and the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) in the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model with Hatsugai-Kohmoto (HK) interactions, specifically exploring the competition between topology and strong correlations.
main_result: The SSH-HK model shows qualitatively distinct behavior from Hubbard models, including a finite response at zero wave vector and a piecewise QFI structure that distinguishes integer from fractional filling sectors, though the QFI remains insensitive to topological transitions in uniform-density regimes.
method: The authors leverage the exact solvability of the HK term to derive closed-form expressions for dynamical susceptibility and use the Random Phase Approximation (RPA) for comparison with Hubbard-type models.
model_or_system: The primary model is the SSH-HK model, which combines the 1D dimerized SSH lattice (with alternating hopping amplitudes v and w) with momentum-diagonal, long-range Hatsugai-Kohmoto interactions.
key_observables: Dynamical spin and charge susceptibilities (Im chi), Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), spectral weight, and topological invariants (Zak phase).
important_parameters: Hopping amplitudes (v, w), interaction strength (U), wave vector (q), frequency (omega), and particle filling (n).
main_assumptions: The comparison models (Hubbard and SSH-Hubbard) are treated within the Random Phase Approximation (RPA); the HK model is treated via its exact solvability.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the evolution of dynamical susceptibility across different frequencies and wave vectors, the piecewise behavior of the QFI across different filling sectors, and the phase boundaries between trivial and topological phases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SSH-HK model, provides an analytical benchmark by comparing it to Hubbard-type models using RPA, derives closed-form expressions for the SSH-HK susceptibility, analyzes the QFI as a probe for filling sectors, and discusses the limitations of these probes in detecting topological transitions.
why_interesting: It provides an analytically tractable platform to study how long-range, momentum-diagonal interactions compete with topological band structures and how entanglement-related quantities like QFI can serve as probes for many-body phases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Imaginary part of the dynamical spin susceptibility of the Hubbard model (energies are given in units of t). Top: ImχRPA(q, ω) for U/t = 0.5 and µ/t = 0.5, corresponding to half filling (µ = U/2), shown for several values of q. Bottom: ImχRPA(q = π, ω) for µ/t = 0.5 and different interaction strengths U/t.

FIG. 2. Imaginary part of the dynamical spin susceptibility of the Hubbard model in the strong-coupling limit (energies are given in units of t). Top: Imχ(q, ω) for U/t = 4 (correspond- ing to J = t) for several values of q. Bottom: Imχ(q = π, ω) for different interaction strengths U/t.

FIG. 3. Imaginary part of the dynamical susceptibility for the SSH–Hubbard model. Top panel: Imχ(q, ω) for fixed in- teraction strength U/w = 0.5 (with w = 1) and several wave vectors q, using v/w = 0.2. Bottom panel: Imχ(q, ω) at fixed wave vector q = π/2 for different values of the Hubbard in- teraction U.

FIG. 4. Imaginary part of the dynamical spin susceptibility of the HK model for several wave vectors q. The chemical potential is set to µ/t = 0.5. Top: weak interaction, U/t = 0.5. Bottom: strong interaction, U/t = 7.
FIG. 5. Imaginary part of the dynamical spin susceptibility ImχSSH−HK(q, ω) as a function of frequency ω for selected wave vectors q. The intercell hopping amplitude is fixed at w = 1.
